coredata.dc:description:
Hypothesis Chemotherapy is effective against oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC), but its utility is hindered by significant side effects and drug resistance. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) offers a less invasive alternative, but challenges remain due to the hydrophobic nature of most photosensitizers (PS), limiting their effectiveness. A novel nano-composite, combining hyaluronic acid-carbon dots (HA-CDs) with cisplatin (DDP), may overcome these limitations by improving drug hydrophobicity and enabling dual-function therapy.
Experiments Hyaluronic acid-carbon dots (HA-CDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using hyaluronic acid as the primary precursor. The resulting HA-CDs were then combined with cisplatin (DDP) to form the HA-CDs-DDP composite, designed to enhance DDP hydrophobicity and serve as both a chemotherapy agent and a PS.
Findings The HA-CDs-DDP composite facilitates self-targeting chemotherapy while acting as an intrinsic photosensitizer, eliminating the need for additional PS. The composite generated reactive oxygen species (ROS), effectively inhibiting tumor growth in vivo. This study demonstrates the potential of HA-CDs-DDP to induce cell death in OSCC via a combined chemotherapy/photodynamic therapy pathway, providing a promising new approach for clinical OSCC treatment.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-cisplatin nano drug delivery system induces the death of oral tongue squamous cell under self-targeting chemical/photodynamic combined therapy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The contributions of these authors to this work are considered equal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemotherapy is effective against oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC), but its utility is hindered by significant side effects and drug resistance. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) offers a less invasive alternative, but challenges remain due to the hydrophobic nature of most photosensitizers (PS), limiting their effectiveness. A novel nano-composite, combining hyaluronic acid-carbon dots (HA-CDs) with cisplatin (DDP), may overcome these limitations by improving drug hydrophobicity and enabling dual-function therapy.

Hyaluronic acid-carbon dots (HA-CDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using hyaluronic acid as the primary precursor. The resulting HA-CDs were then combined with cisplatin (DDP) to form the HA-CDs-DDP composite, designed to enhance DDP hydrophobicity and serve as both a chemotherapy agent and a PS.

The HA-CDs-DDP composite facilitates self-targeting chemotherapy while acting as an intrinsic photosensitizer, eliminating the need for additional PS. The composite generated reactive oxygen species (ROS), effectively inhibiting tumor growth in vivo. This study demonstrates the potential of HA-CDs-DDP to induce cell death in OSCC via a combined chemotherapy/photodynamic therapy pathway, providing a promising new approach for clinical OSCC treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Of late years, the incidence of oral cancer has been slowly declining in some developed countries. However, globally, the incidence is gradually increasing due to common oral habits such as smoking, drinking, betel nut chewing, and others. This trend is contributing to a rise in both morbidity and mortality rates related to oral cancer. Of these, oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) stands out as the most predominant form [1]. In cases of early oral cancer without lymph node metastasis, it is preferable to opt for either surgery or radiotherapy alone. However, for middle and advanced patients, with the enlargement of the tumor, often accompanied by regional lymph node metastasis, it is necessary not only to deal with local lesions, but also to expand the scope of surgery, hence, the surgical method is no longer suitable.

In recent years, chemotherapy for oral cancer has developed rapidly and has become one of the main components of comprehensive treatment. The type of administration has changed from single-drug to combined-drug therapy, and the mode of administration has shifted from traditional palliative chemotherapy to neoadjuvant chemotherapy before surgery or radiotherapy, sensitization prior to radiotherapy, etc. Systemic chemotherapy has a limited curative effect on oral cancer due to its high side effects, inability to eradicate cancer, and the inherent drug resistance in the human body. These factors significantly hinder its further development in clinical practice [2,3]. Cisplatin (DDP) is an antitumor chemotherapeutic drug that induces DNA damage in cancer cells by cross-linking with DNA, activating iron-induced autophagy [4,5], making it a highly effective clinical chemotherapeutic drug [6,7]. However, DDP exerts cytotoxic effects on tumor cells but also causes significant systemic cytotoxicity, resulting in widespread toxic side effects [8]. On the one hand, this is attributed to its poor specificity towards tumor cells, which can lead to potential damage to normal tissues. On the other hand, its low water solubility exacerbates the issue. Both factors are crucial limitations to the clinical application of DDP. Consequently, the focus of research has shifted towards addressing how to mitigate the toxic side effects of DDP, enhance its specificity for tumor tissues, and improve its anti-tumor efficacy. In recent years, ligand-receptor-mediated transport systems have attracted significant attention for their potential to enhance targeted therapy with nanomedicines. As a result, the development and construction of effective drug carriers that can enhance the specific targeting of chemotherapy drugs, reduce toxic side effects during delivery, and control the release rate of these drugs have become critically significant for improving the therapeutic efficacy of DDP in chemotherapy.

Compared to normal oral epithelial cells, The upregulation of CD44 on the surface of OSCC cells presents a viable target for precision-targeted therapeutic interventions [9]. CD44, a transmembrane glycoprotein, exhibits widespread expression across diverse cell types and assumes a pivotal role in numerous biological processes. CD44 plays a major role in cell adhesion, cell migration, signal transduction, cell proliferation, and tumor metastasis. CD44 is a cell surface marker that can be utilized to identify specific cells. In addition, CD44 is also a prominent topic in oncology. In many types of tumors, CD44 is considered a marker for tumor stem cells. Tumor stem cells are a type of cell endowed with the capacity for self-renewal, differentiation, development, and drug resistance. Therefore, CD44 has become an important marker for studying tumor stem cells. It is a significant candidate receptor in the development of cancer chemotherapy and molecularly targeted therapies. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) presents a non-invasive treatment strategy reliant upon photosensitizers (PS). Due to its precise efficacy, minimal trauma, low toxicity, and repeatable treatment effect, PDT is undergoing rapid development for both cancer diagnosis and treatment [10]. PS can accumulate in tumor tissues and, in the presence of endogenous oxygen, can be activated by light sources of specific wavelengths. This activation initiates the generation of cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), culminating in the demise of cancer cells [11–15]. However, PS for clinical application should be non-toxic, selectively concentrated in cancer tissues, and activated by light with strong tissue penetration (600–800 nm). PS is not only required to have long photochemical spectral wavelengths, but it can also penetrate the cell and bind closely to the photosensitive matrix within the cell. Although thousands of different sorts of PS have been studied, the process of most PS is complex, and the production of ROS is low. Therefore, discovering stable and efficient PS remains a clinical challenge. Although traditional treatments have improved, the 5-year survival rate remains low, ranging from 30 % to 40 %[16]. Therefore, it is necessary to research new materials for treating OSCC. Researchers led by Xiaoyou Xu from the University of South Carolina in the United States reported the production of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) utilizing the arc discharge methodology. They discovered Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) emitting intense fluorescence for the first time during the purification process of the products using electrophoresis [17]. CQD, alternatively referred to as Carbon Dots (CDs), constitute a category of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials distinguished by their exceptional fluorescence characteristics. They have garnered increasing attention in cancer treatment [18,19] due to their outstanding biological compatibility [20], good water solubility [21,22], low toxicity, and many other advantages [23–25]. However, for tumor therapy, most CDs lack targeted recognition and tumor treatment functions, which significantly reduces their therapeutic efficacy. Due to the complex preparation, research on CDs with photodynamic functionality is limited.

Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a polysaccharide acid that was first isolated from the bovine eye vitreous body by Meyer, a professor of ophthalmology at Columbia University in 1934. HA, with its unique molecular structure and physicochemical properties, assumes a pivotal role in numerous physiological functions within the body. It finds extensive application in biomedicine, particularly in cancer treatment, by virtue of its capability to specifically target CD44 receptors on the surface of cancer cells [26–28], facilitating early cancer diagnosis. In addition, HA can act as a PS and produce ROS under light conditions [29]. Studies have shown that CDs synthesized using HA as a raw material can retain these functions on the HA surface, acting as PS to produce ROS under light conditions, thereby killing cancer cells. Furthermore, given their increased specific surface area, excellent biocompatibility, and abundance of amino, carboxyl, and other functional groups, CDs can be used as carriers of certain drugs [30,31] with therapeutic potential. Compared with free drugs. The nanodrug delivery system of CDs offers improved drug solubility, higher therapeutic efficiency, and can reduce side effects to achieve targeted chemotherapy [32–34]. Hence, we can utilize CDs in combination with the chemotherapy drug DDP to achieve a dual-targeted effect of chemotherapy and PDT on tumors [35,36].

By integrating the unique properties of CDs with practical applications, the preparation of functionalized CDs serves dual purposes. On one hand, it can mitigate the toxicity generated by inorganic nanomaterials within biological organisms. On the other hand, within material systems based on CDs, various functional substances can be labeled. This, from a practical standpoint, achieves a certain level of method optimization, improvement, or fulfillment of specific functionalities in particular applications.

What's more, the experiments manifested that administering chemotherapy and PDT in close succession or simultaneously should synergistically enhance efficacy in both in vitro and in vivo settings. Considering these discoveries alongside the distinct mechanisms of action exhibited by PDT and DDP, we conducted research on the therapeutic potential of these two approaches, both in laboratory cell cultures and in live animal models specifically focusing on OSCC. Using HA as a carbon source, we synthesized HA-CDs through a simple hydrothermal method and grafted them with the chemotherapy drug DDP to construct a nanoparticle drug delivery system (HA-CDs-DDP) integrating targeted chemotherapy and PDT. The drug delivery system possesses two distinctive characteristics: photo-induced luminescence and nanoscale drug carriers. These features not only enable the specific delivery of drugs to cancer cell sites but also allow for the observation of cellular status and drug delivery processes through fluorescence imaging. Therefore, investigating drug delivery systems based on CDs is of significant importance for the treatment, monitoring, and tracking of drug release processes in cancer. We conducted in vitro cell experiments to experimentally verify the targeted chemotherapy and photodynamic effects when exposed to 660 nm laser irradiation. Animal experiments have also confirmed its superior antitumor effects compared to either chemotherapy or PDT alone, even resulting in tumor cell death.

HA, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), and quinine sulfate were obtained from Macklin Company (Shanghai, China). DCFH-DA was sourced from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). DDP was purchased from MCE Company (Shanghai, China). DMEM medium (high sugar), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and pancreatic enzyme were produced by Gibco, USA. Penicillin-streptomycin-amphotericin is from Beijing Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. PBS (1X) and a cell apoptosis detection kit were acquired from Meilunbio Biological (Dalian, China). The EDU cell proliferation detection kit was sourced from Ruibo Biotech (Guangzhou, China). The Calcein-AM/PI staining kit was provided by Bestbio (Shanghai, China). Reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection kit was sourced from Beyotime Biotechnology Corporation (Shanghai, China). DAPI staining solution was acquired from Boster Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Human oral tongue squamous cells (Cal-27 cells) were obtained from the China Center for Type Culture Collection (CCTCC). Human oral mucosal keratinocytes (HOK cells) were obtained from Sciencell Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Milli-Q Advance A10 supplied ultrapure water for all experiments. All chemical reagents were used in experiments without any additional processing.

Morphological features and particle sizes of HA-CDs were analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) from FEI (United States). The fluorescence spectrophotometer was obtained from Edinburgh Instruments Ltd. (United Kingdom), and the X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was from Bruker Corporation (Germany). Drug loading experiments were conducted using a DF-01 OTP constant temperature heating magnetic stirrer manufactured by Shanghai Linbel Instrument Co., Ltd. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP were analyzed using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer manufactured by Thermo Fisher. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted utilizing an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The Ultraviolet-Visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra of HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP were recorded with equipment supplied by Shanghai Metash Instrument Co., Ltd. Cell imaging was conducted utilizing an Olympus laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) in Japan. Cell apoptosis was analyzed using a flow cytometer manufactured by Agilent Technologies (United States). BAX, Caspase-3, and BCL-2 antibodies were obtained from Huabio (Zhejiang, China).

Initially, a straightforward and universally applicable hydrothermal method was utilized for the synthesis HA-CDs, based on established literature protocols [37–41]. Specifically, 1 g of sodium hyaluronate was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water within a high-pressure reaction kettle. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to a vacuum drying oven at 200 °C for a 6 h. After natural cooling, the solution was centrifuged using a high-speed centrifuge (10,000 rpm, 15 min), followed by filtration through a disposable microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Following this, the resulting solution was dialyzed in ultra-pure water using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. Subsequently, the sample was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain solid and pure HA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the HA-CDs-DDP drug delivery system
ce:section.ce:para: The HA-CDs-DDP nanocomposite material was synthesized by reference to the synthetic method of HA-CDs-DOX. 1 mg/mL solution of HA-CDs (5 mL) and DDP (5 mL) were combined in a glass bottle and stirred for a duration of 12 h at ambient temperature employing a magnetic stirring apparatus. Later, the solution underwent a 24-hour dialysis process using a dialysis bag for the follow-up experiments. Following dialysis, the UV-Vis spectrum of the solution external to the dialysis bag was recorded using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer.

Firstly, the standard curve of absorbance change with concentration was drawn according to different concentrations of DDP solution, and then the content of DDP was calculated according to the absorbance of DDP at 210 nm and the drug loading rate was calculated.

A dialysis bag containing 10 mL of the HA-CDs-DDP solution was placed into a beaker with 100 mL of PBS. Every 1 h, 5 mL of liquid from the beaker was measured for absorbance. The drug release amount for each time interval was determined using the standard curve of the DDP solution, and a drug release curve was plotted.

This experiment utilized Cal-27 cells provided by CCTCC (Wuhan, China) and HOK cells provided by Sciencell (Shanghai, China). The cells were grown in DMEM medium containing 12 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin-amphotericin, within a cell culture incubator maintained at 37 °C with 5 % CO₂.

In this experiment, DPBF was first mixed with HA-CDs-DDP to detect the extracellular ROS generation capacity of HA-CDs-DDP. DPBF is a fluorescence probe for singlet oxygen, highly specific for singlet oxygen, which can form endoperoxides and decompose into 1,2-diphenylmethane. Once bound to ROS, DPBF is irreversibly oxidized, and the absorption intensity at UV-Vis (around 410 nm) rapidly decreases, thus indicating ROS generation. This method was employed to assess the vitro photodynamic efficacy of HA-CDs-DDP in vitro. Initially, 1 mL HA-CDs-DDP(7ug/mL) was mixed with 10 mM DPBF, then the solution's vertical surface was exposed to a 660 nm laser at a power density of 1 W/cm², with exposure intervals of 1 min. The absorbance changes at 410 nm were assessed utilizing UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy. Simultaneously, DPBF surfaces were irradiated in the same manner as the control group.

Following this, a commercially available ROS detection kit employing the fluorescence probe DCFH-DA was utilized for the quantification of ROS levels. DCFH-DA does not exhibit inherent fluorescence and can easily penetrate cell membranes. Once inside the cells, it can be hydrolyzed by the esterase enzyme present in the cell. DCFH, which is non-fluorescent, undergoes oxidation by intracellular ROS to generate fluorescent DCF. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity can be utilized to analyze the concentration of ROS within the cells. In this investigation, Cal-27 cells were initially incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, solutions containing HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP were introduced, and the cells were subsequently cultured for an additional 24 h. Following this, the solution was exposed to laser irradiation for a duration of 5 min. Incubation continued with 2 mL of diluted DCFH-DA instead of cell medium. After rinsing the cells with serum-free culture medium, they were photographed by the fluorescence microscope.

Cal-27 cells and HOK cells were selected as the experimental cell lines. The two cell varieties were seeded onto slides within 24-well plates and cultivated for 24 h. Afterward, the culture medium was aspirated, and the wells were refreshed with 600 μL of high-glucose DMEM medium containing HA-CDs or HA-CDs-DDP for another 24 h. Subsequently, PBS washing, tissue fixation, and DAPI staining were conducted, followed by the LSCM. To further investigate the drug uptake by Cal-27 cells, the cells were cultured with HA-CDs-DDP in the same manner and then detected using LSCM at different time points.

The hemolysis assay was used to detect the presence and degree of hemolysis, assessing the biotoxicity of the substance. Whole blood was obtained from healthy BALB/C mice in anticoagulant tubes. Different concentrations of HA-CDs (200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μg/mL), ultrapure water, and PBS solution were mixed with blood cells. The samples were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h, followed by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 15 min. Subsequently, they were positioned horizontally for photography using a smartphone.

Cal-27 cells and HOK cells were used as template cells. The cytotoxicity of synthetic HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP was evaluated through the CCK-8 assay. After 24 h of cell culture, adding 100 μL of HA-CDs (0, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μg/mL) and HA-CDs-DDP (with DDP concentrations of 0, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 μg/mL) solutions to each well for another 24 h. 10 μL of the prepared CCK-8 solution was introduced into each well, followed by an additional 3 h incubation period. Subsequently, the supernatant was moved to a fresh plate for absorbance measurement, allowing for the assessment of cytotoxicity.

EDU, a thymidine analog, replaces thymidine (T) in replicating DNA during cell proliferation, enabling the rapid and accurate detection of cell DNA replication activity. Cal-27 cells were plated into 24-well plates fitted with clamps and treated with HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (with a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL). Subsequently, adding 200 μL of EDU culture medium (50 μM) to each well for 2 h. Afterward, the culture medium was discarded, and the cells were rinsed with PBS 1–2 times. Each well was then treated with 200 μL of tissue fixative for 30 min, followed by 5 min with 200 μL of glycine decolorizing solution, 5 min with PBS, 10 min of permeabilization with 600 μL of penetrant, and a final wash with PBS. Subsequently, add 600 μL of 1× Apollo staining reaction solution to each well and incubate for 30 min without light. After removing the reaction solution, add 600 μL of washing solution for three washes, and then discard the washing solution. Finally, add 600 μL of 1× Hoechst reaction solution to each well for cell culture, and capture cell images using a confocal microscope.

The drug's inhibitory effect on cell migration ability was first identified using the cell scratch method to assess the potential toxicity of nanomaterials. Firstly, the back of a 6-well plate was uniformly marked. Then, scratches were made when the cells reached confluence, followed by the addition of solutions containing HA-CDs (1 mg/mL), DDP (7 μg/mL), and HA-CDs-DDP (with a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) in serum-free medium configuration, respectively. Photographs were taken and the change in scratch width was recorded at different times after incubation.

The migration ability of Cal-27 cells was assessed using the transwell migration assay. HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP solutions (with a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) containing 10 % FBS were added to a 24-well plate. Subsequently, 100 μL of cell suspension in serum-free medium was introduced into the transwell chambers, which were then placed into the plate. Crystal violet staining was performed for 10 min after 24 h of cell incubation, followed by rinsing with PBS three times. After air-drying, the experimental results were recorded.

We assessed the phototoxicity and dark toxicity of HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP using the CCK-8 assay. Cal-27 cells were subjected to incubation with HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (containing a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) in complete culture medium for 24 h. Afterward, the plate was taken out, and 100 μL of HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP solutions into each well for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were then exposed to a 660 nm laser at an intensity of 1 W/cm². Adding 10 μL of the completed CCK-8 solution to each well, the culture was then incubated for 3 h, the supernatant was transferred into a new 96-well plate for absorbance measurement to assess cytotoxicity. The operation was the same for the control group, except there was no light.

Cells were inoculated into 24-well plates and exposed to HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (containing a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) for 24 h, followed by laser irradiation for 5 min. In addition to the light-untreated control group, all other conditions were kept constant. Following an additional 6-h incubation, Calcein-AM/PI staining was conducted for 30 min, and the samples were subsequently photographed under a fluorescence microscope.

Apoptosis was evaluated utilizing the Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis kit. Annexin V-FITC labels early apoptotic cells, whereas PI binds to late apoptotic or dead cells. Once cells reached confluence, HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (containing a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) were introduced and incubated for 24 h. Following incubation, the cells underwent laser irradiation for 5 min, with the control group undergoing the same conditions but without light exposure. After incubating for an additional 6 h, the cells were trypsinized, washed with PBS, and then resuspended. Subsequently, they were cultured with Annexin V-FITC and PI for further experimentation. Subsequently, flow cytometry was employed for detection within 1 h.

The JC-1 mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit comprises a fluorescence probe that utilizes JC-1 detection to detect cell changes rapidly, sensitively, and accurately. The reduction in mitochondrial membrane potential is an indicator of the early stages of cell apoptosis. Using a laser confocal microscope, the transformation from red to green fluorescence is observed. Cal-27 cells were cultured with HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (with a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) in complete culture medium for 24 h, then exposed to 660 nm (1 W/cm2) laser irradiation for 5 min. The control group was subjected to the same conditions, except without light exposure. After further incubation for 6 h, the medium was discarded, and 200 μL of freshly prepared cell culture medium containing JC-1 staining solution were added, followed by an incubation period of 20 min. Finally, the supernatant was aspirated, and the cells were washed with JC-1 staining buffer before observation using a laser confocal microscope.

Cal-27 cells were cultured in complete medium with HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (containing a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) for 24 h. Subsequently, each well was exposed to laser treatment and continued to culture for an additional 6 h. The control group underwent the same conditions, except without laser irradiation. The cells were then scraped off using a cell scraper containing PBS. The cells were lysed with RAPI lysis buffer for a duration of 30 min, and the BCA method was employed to determine the protein content quantitatively. Subsequently, electrophoresis, transfer, and blocking were performed. The target proteins were then incubated overnight with Caspase-3, BCL-2, and BAX, followed by the addition of secondary antibodies. Finally, an exposure was taken using a chemiluminescence imager, and Image J software was utilized for grayscale analysis and statistical analysis.

RT-PCR (Reverse Transcription-Polymerase Chain Reaction) serves as a method for assessing gene expression levels within cells. Primers were designed and synthesized (Supplement Table 1), and samples were prepared. Cal-27 cells were cultured with HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (with a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) in complete culture medium for 24 h. Subsequently, RNA was extracted, followed by reverse transcription to generate cDNA. Subsequently, a PCR reaction system containing fluorescence-conjugated probes was prepared. Finally, PCR reactions were conducted using a quantitative PCR instrument.

Cal-27 cells were cultured with HA-CDs, DDP, and HA-CDs-DDP (containing a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) in complete culture medium for 24 h. Following exposure to a 660 nm laser (1 W/cm²) for 5 min, they were further incubated for an additional 6 h. The control group underwent the same conditions, except without laser irradiation. After fixation with tissue fixative for 30 min and blocking with BSA for 30 min, primary antibodies against Caspase-3, BCL-2, and BAX were applied overnight, followed by the application of the secondary antibody for 1 h. The cell nuclei underwent staining with DAPI solution for 30 min. The intensity of cell fluorescence was observed using confocal laser microscopy.

BALB/C nude mice required for this experiment were provided by Beijing Victory Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The mice were approximately 5 weeks old, weighing 14–17 g, and were kept in a sterile environment. The water and food were sterilized using high-pressure sterilization before being fed. Tumor-bearing mice were generated by inoculating Cal-27 cells (3 × 106) into the right axilla of nude mice. The nude mice were subsequently randomly allocated into 7 groups (PBS + laser, HA-CDs, DDP, HA-CDs-DDP, HA-CDs + laser, DDP + laser, HA-CDs-DDP + laser group) when the average tumor diameter reached approximately 5 mm, with 5 mice in each group. The mice were given 100 μL of the corresponding drug (containing a DDP concentration of 7 μg/mL) in the tail vein every 3 days, and the laser group was irradiated with a laser 12 h after each administration. Throughout the treatment period, the body weight and tumor volume of the mice were monitored every 3 days, and photographs of the mice were taken every 7 days.
All mice were sacrificed after 21 days. The organs were excised, fixed, dehydrated, embedded, sliced, and stained with H&E.

Upon completion of the therapy, euthanasia was performed on all mice. Tumor tissues were excised, fixed, photographed, weighed, embedded, sliced, stained with H&E, subjected to TDT-mediated dUTP Notch End Labeling (TUNEL), and immunohistochemically stained (IHC), and finally observed under a microscope.
Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way ANOVA in GraphPad Prism 9, with a significance threshold set at P < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and property test of HA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal method was first employed to prepare HA-CDs by reference to the literature, showing promising potential for clinical applications. The microstructure and diameter of HA-CDs particles were examined via a transmission electron microscope (TEM) ( Fig. 1a), showing a uniform spherical distribution with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, akin to the structure found in graphene [42]. The size distribution of HA-CDs' diameter was primarily concentrated in the range of 2–5 nm, with an average size of approximately 3.72 nm (Fig. 1b).

The XRD pattern of HA-CDs displayed a prominent diffraction peak at 2θ = 25°, suggesting that the HA-CDs primarily consisted of amorphous carbon ( Fig. 2a). Subsequently, the chemical composition and surface characteristics of nanomaterials were investigated. FTIR spectroscopy was employed to detect the main functional groups present in HA-CDs (Fig. 2b). A broad absorption peak observed at 3440 cm−1 corresponds to OH/NH stretching vibrations [43]. The absorption peak at 2962 cm−1 is indicative of the stretching vibration of C-H [44]. Peaks located at 1666 cm−1 and 1419 cm−1 can be assigned to CO [45] and C-N [46] stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption peak at 1110 cm−1 arises from the stretching vibration of C-O-C [47]. These characteristic peaks are in proximity to those of HA, suggesting that the synthesized HA-CDs may preserve some properties of HA. The FTIR spectrum indicates that the surface contains hydrophilic groups such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl, which provide good water solubility. This makes them suitable carriers for the preparation of functional CDs. The total XPS spectrum of HA-CDs revealed peaks at 285 eV, 399 eV, and 532 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d, e, and f display the contours of C1s, N1s, and O1s for HA-CDs. In the C1s spectrum, four distinct peaks at 284.6, 285.3, 286.6, and 288.7 eV can be observed, corresponding to CC, C-C, C-N/C-O and CO, respectively. In the N1s spectrum, two signals at 399.4 eV and 400.1 eV are attributable to C-N-C and N-H bonds. In the O1s spectrum, signals at 531.9 eV and 533.0 eV can be attributable to CO and C-OH[48]. Appropriate energy levels are of crucial significance for the PS. Building upon this foundation, the electronic structure of HA-CDs was further researched through optical characterization. The UV-Vis spectra of HA-CDs exhibited a distinctive peak at 263 nm, close to the absorption peak of HA (Fig. 2g). Fig. 2h and i depict the emission-related spectra, fluorescence excitation spectrum, and emission spectrum of HA-CDs. The most intense fluorescence emission is observed at an excitation wavelength of 392 nm. At this optimal excitation wavelength, the emission peaks at 495 nm, signifying that the optimal emission wavelength of HA-CDs is 495 nm.
A standard reference for fluorescence emission, quinine sulfate (0.1 mol/L of H2SO4) with a concentration of 0.56, was utilized.

The total XPS spectrum of HA-CDs-DDP and the XPS spectrum of DDP indicate that the composite contains the Pt4f peaks of DDP, preliminary indicating the successful loading of DDP ( Fig. 3a, b). The UV-Vis absorption spectra revealed that HA-CDs exhibited a distinctive peak at 265 nm, DDP showed a distinctive peak at 203 nm, while the spectrum of HA-CDs-DDP displayed two absorption peaks at 203 nm and 265 nm simultaneously, aligning with the absorption peaks of HA-CDs and DDP, confirming the successful loading of DDP (Fig. 3c). The FTIR spectrum of the reactants and the complex revealed that the peak around 3394 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH-NH. Peaks around 1558 cm−1, 1419 cm−1, and 1033 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations associated with CO, C-N, and C-O-C bonds. The peak around 802 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of Pt-N, indicating the successful loading of DDP (Fig. 3d). These characterizations demonstrate the successful synthesis of HA-CDs-DDP.

According to the UV-Vis spectrum and the standard curve of DDP (Fig. 3e), the loading rate of DDP is calculated to be 61.05 %. Our research found that the DOX release rate remains relatively stable under different pH conditions of 7.4 and 6.5. At pH 6.5, the release rate reaches 63.8 % within 12 h, while at pH 7.4, it reaches 50.7 % within the same time frame. Under both conditions, DOX release reaches approximately 80 % after 36 h and then stabilizes.(Fig. 3f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro performance characterization of HA-CDs-DDP
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, HA-CDs-DDP are capable of generating ROS upon exposure to a 660 nm laser, which proved lethal to Cal-27 cells. Here, DPBF was used as a tracer agent to verify the extracellular ROS generation capability of HA-CDs-DDP [49,50]. Fig. 4a and b represent the visible-ultraviolet spectra of DPBF and DPBF + HA-CDs-DDP, respectively. The results indicate that under laser irradiation at 660 nm (1 W/cm²), the absorbance of DPBF at 410 nm remained relatively constant between 0 and 10 min, while the absorbance of DPBF + HA-CDs-DDP at 410 nm gradually decreased over time. This suggests the generation of ROS, confirming the excellent photodynamic performance of HA-CDs-DDP.

Furthermore, using DCFH as a detection reagent, the intracellular capacity for generating ROS of different materials was evaluated [51–53]. Fig. 4c illustrates the levels of ROS expression for different materials before and after laser irradiation. As shown, HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP displayed weak green fluorescence before laser irradiation, as HA-CDs themselves emit green fluorescence. After laser irradiation, both the HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP groups exhibited strong green fluorescence, showing the generation of ROS. In contrast, the control and DDP groups showed no fluorescence before or after light exposure, demonstrating no photodynamic effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the activity of nanocomposites in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption of a drug by tumor cells plays a strong part in its treatment effectiveness. To verify the uptake ability of HA-CDs-DDP by Cal-27 cells, we conducted confocal fluorescence imaging. HOK cells with low expression of CD44 and Cal-27 cells with high expression of CD44 were selected as experimental cells, and the capacity of cells to uptake HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP was observed with laser confocal microscopy. Cell nuclei were stained using DAPI. The red channels represent the cytoskeleton, while the green channels represent HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP (Fig. 4d). The laser confocal imaging results of Cal-27 cells co-cultured with HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP for 24 h show that HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP are mainly distributed in the cytoplasm. The green channel exhibits strong fluorescence, indicating the effective uptake of HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP by Cal-27 cells. In addition, the laser confocal imaging of HOK cells co-cultured with HA-CDs and HA-CDs-DDP for 24 h indicated a relatively weaker fluorescence intensity in the green channel (Supplementary Fig. S1). These findings suggest that Cal-27 cells show improved uptake through targeting. To further investigate the uptake of HA-CDs-DDP by Cal-27 cells, laser confocal imaging was conducted after co-culturing for 6, 12, and 24 h. As shown in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence strength in the green channel consistently increased with time, indicating a time-dependent uptake of the substance by the cells. Fig. 4f demonstrates the hemolysis of blood cells in HA-CDs. As shown in the figure, they are not soluble in different concentrations of HA-CDs (200–1000 μg/mL), indicating that HA-CDs do not possess hemolytic ability and exhibit excellent biocompatibility.

Inspired by the superior ROS generation capability of HA-CDs-DDP, we subsequently evaluated the therapeutic effect of the nanocomposite at the cellular level. The CCK-8 method was initially used to assess the cytotoxic effects of various materials on Cal-27 cells and HOK cells before or after laser irradiation ( Fig. 5a). Results indicated that, in the absence of laser irradiation, HA-CDs exhibited minimal cytotoxicity to both types of cells, with cell viability exceeding 90 % even when the concentration reached 1000 μg/mL. Conversely, the cytotoxic effect of HA-CDs-DDP on HOK cells was greater compared to that on Cal-27 cells, due to its ability to target and recognize cancer cells. For Cal-27 cells, HA-CDs-DDP proved to be more lethal than DDP alone due to its targeting ability. Under laser irradiation, DDP did not exhibit a photodynamic effect, and the cell vitality did not change significantly. In contrast, the cell vitality of the HA-CDs group decreased significantly. By contrast, HA-CDs-DDP had the most lethal effect on cells, with more than 75 % of cells dying. These findings indicate that the synergistic effect of chemotherapy and PDT on Cal-27 cells is significantly superior to monotherapy. In addition, The IC50 value of the free DDP group was 7 μg/mL after incubation for 24 h (Supplementary Fig. S2). Therefore, we chose 7 μg/mL DDP as the concentration for subsequent experiments.

In order to simulate the impact of HA-CDs-DDP on the in vitro growth of tumors, we used Cal-27 cells and added EDU (Fig. 5b). In contrast to the control group, there was a marked reduction in the quantity of proliferating cells observed after treatment with DDP and HA-CDs-DDP. The decrease was even less in the HA-CDs-DDP group. This indicates that the drug-loaded composite HA-CDs-DDP can better inhibit cell proliferation than using DDP alone. This is attributed to the targeted action of HA-CDs, enabling Cal-27 cells to absorb more HA-CDs-DDP. Additionally, the figure still shows a significant number of proliferating cells in the HA-CDs group, confirming the biocompatibility of HA-CDs.

Then, the impact of HA-CDs-DDP on the migration ability of Cal-27 cells was evaluated by assessing changes in scratch width (Fig. 5c). The scratch width of the HA-CDs group was smaller at 12 h and significantly reduced at 24 h, similar to the control group, indicating good biocompatibility of HA-CDs. At 24 h, the scratch width slightly decreased in the DDP group. The scratch width in the HA-CDs-DDP group remained almost unchanged after 24 h. Additionally, the HA-CDs-DDP group exhibited a considerable amount of cell death and a noticeable decrease in cell density. The results indicate that HA-CDs-DDP is more effective in inhibiting cell migration than DDP.

To further validate the suppressive impact of HA-CDs-DDP on the migratory capability of Cal-27 cells, the quantity of Cal-27 cells passing through the compartments was observed. According to Fig. 5d, in comparison to the control group, the number of cells traversed significantly decreased in the DDP group (top right) and the HA-CDs-DDP group (bottom right). Moreover, the HA-CDs-DDP group demonstrated fewer cells traversed than the DDP group. This suggests that HA-CDs-DDP significantly inhibits the migration of Cal-27 cells.

Following the demonstration of the dark toxicity of HA-CDs-DDP, the cell survival status was determined using the Calcein-AM/PI staining test to study the cytotoxic effects of HA-CDs-DDP on Cal-27 cells under light conditions. The result in Fig. 5e shows that the HA-CDs group exhibited almost no cell death in the absence of light; However, both the DDP and HA-CDs-DDP groups had some dead cells, with the latter showing more dead cells. Under laser irradiation, the number of dead cells did not change significantly in the DDP group, indicating its lack of photodynamic ability. In contrast, both the HA-CDs-treated and HA-CDs-DDP-treated groups showed a notable rise in the number of dead cells after laser irradiation, with the latter group having almost all cells dead. This further confirms the photodynamic effect of HA-CDs-DDP.

To quantitatively investigate the apoptosis of cells induced by HA-CDs-DDP, Annexin V-FITC/PI staining was performed on Cal-27 cells. The findings depicted in Fig. 5f indicate that, when compared to the control group, the HA-CDs group did not display a significant alteration in apoptosis rate, indicating that HA-CDs have minimal cytotoxicity. The apoptosis rate of the HA-CDs-DDP group was slightly higher compared to the DDP and DDP + laser groups, indicating that the carrier complex promotes cell apoptosis more effectively than the DDP alone group. In contrast, the killing effect of the HA-CDs + light group was more pronounced. The HA-CDs-DDP + light group exhibited the highest rates of both early and late apoptosis, with the early apoptosis rate showing a more pronounced change (30.68 %), indicating the maximum cytotoxic effect of the drug complex combined with light on Cal-27 cells.

The stability of mitochondrial function is a crucial foundation for the proliferation and reproduction of cancer cells. The impact of drugs or the elevation in intracellular ROS can cause the mitochondrial membrane potential to decrease, which signifies the early apoptosis of cells. Given the link between cell apoptosis with mitochondrial dysfunction, we employed JC-1 staining to assess mitochondrial damage, where red fluorescence denotes intact mitochondria, while green fluorescence signifies damaged mitochondria. Fig. 5g shows that the proportion of red and green fluorescence in the HA-CDs-DDP + laser group has the most significant difference, indicating the depolarization of mitochondria. Once again, early apoptosis was demonstrated.

After demonstrating the promotion of apoptosis by HA-CDs-DDP at the cellular level, we further validated the photodynamic effects of HA-CDs-DDP at the molecular level through immunofluorescence, WB and RT-PCR analyses. Laser confocal imaging reveals increased fluorescence of pro-apoptotic genes Caspase-3 and BAX and decreased fluorescence of the anti-apoptotic gene BCL-2 post-photodynamic treatment, confirming a robust photodynamic effect consistent with cellular experimental results ( Fig. 6a). Statistical analysis indicates a significant difference (Supplementary Fig. S3). Fig. 6b presents the WB results, showing a significant increase in BAX and Caspase-3 expression and a notable decrease in BCL-2 expression after irradiation. Statistical analysis indicates a significant difference (Fig. 6c). Fig. 6d displays the expression of Caspase-3, BCL-2, and BAX after photodynamic treatment at the gene level, which is consistent with the WB experimental results.

Despite the promising aspects of nanoparticles in cancer treatment, concerns about their biocompatibility still exist. Upon completion of the treatment, all mice were euthanized, and their organs were extracted for H&E staining to assess the biosecurity of the materials (Supplementary Fig. S4). The calculated result shows that there was no significant organ damage in any of the groups, indicating that HA-CD-DDP exhibits good biocompatibility in vivo treatment.

Encouraged by the in vitro biological compatibility and pronounced cell lethality of HA-CDs-DDP under 660 nm light exposure, we subsequently studied its efficacy in suppressing tumor growth in BALB/C mice implanted with Cal-27 cells. ( Fig. 7a). In a 21-day in vivo experiment, the diameter of tumors was measured every third day. The control group and HA-CDs group exhibited the fastest and nearly identical growth, suggesting excellent biocompatibility of HA-CDs. In comparison to the control group, tumor volumes in the DDP group and DDP + laser group increased at a slower rate, suggesting a certain inhibiting effects on tumors. However, the growth rate of the HA-CDs-DDP group was slower than that of the DDP alone group, underscoring the superiority of HA-CDs-DDP over DDP alone. The tumor volume of the HA-CDs + laser group slightly decreased on the 6th day and then slowly increased, showing signs of recurrence. The group treated with HA-CDs-DDP + laser showed a gradual decrease in tumor volume starting from the 3rd day, eventually becoming smaller than the initial volume by the 21st day. This indicates a significant killing effect on tumor cells. Figs. 7b and 7c display photos and weights of the tumors post-treatment, with the smallest size and weight observed in the photodynamic treatment group. This aligns with the findings in Fig. 7a, suggesting that the combination of chemotherapy and PDT yields the most effective treatment outcome. The weight of tumor-bearing mice was measured every 3 days during the 21-day treatment period, and the results are presented in Fig. 7d. The weight of mice in all groups showed a gradual increase, suggesting that the material had a good biosafety.

During the treatment period, tumor-bearing mice were photographed every 7 days to record tumor size, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S5. Scabs appeared at all tumor sites in each group. The control group and the HA-CDs group demonstrated similarly rapid growth rates, indicating that HA-CDs have good biosafety. Tumor volume increased at a certain rate in the DDP and DDP + laser groups, but more slowly than in the control group, indicating that DDP has certain inhibiting effects on tumors. The HA-CDs-DDP group had a smaller tumor volume than the DDP group alone, possibly due to the targeted effect facilitated by HA-CDs-DDP. The tumor size of the HA-CDs + laser group slightly decreased initially and then gradually increased, indicating that photodynamic treatment was not able to completely eliminate the tumor. The HA-CDs-DDP + laser group significantly reduced the tumor volume, indicating that chemotherapy combined with PDT can effectively kill tumors.

Upon concluding the therapy, all mice were put to death, and the tumors were subsequently subjected to H&E, IHC, and TUNEL staining to assess the treatment efficacy of the materials. H&E staining analysis revealed a substantial decrease in tumor cell density in the HA-CDs-DDP + laser group. The cell nuclei appeared lighter in color, and numerous fibrous strands were visible ( Fig. 8), demonstrating the excellent photodynamic therapeutic effect of HA-CDs-DDP. IHC was employed for the detection of the relevant molecule (Ki-67) expression in the tumors from different groups. In this experiment, the positive signal is represented by the color brown. In comparison with the control and HA-CDs groups, the density of brown color in the other experimental groups decreased successively, with the HA-CDs-DDP + laser group showing the least amount. This further explains the anti-tumor effects of nanocomposites in vivo. Meanwhile, TUNEL showed the brightest green fluorescence among tumors treated with HA-CDs-DDP + light. This finding aligns with the results obtained from both H&E and IHC staining analyses.

OSCC is a common head and neck malignancy that presents numerous challenges and opportunities in treatment [54]. Despite advancements in traditional treatment methods such as surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy, the outlook for OSCC remains poor, characterized by a low five-year survival rate. Issues such as tumor recurrence and metastasis, treatment resistance, and side effects on normal tissues are pressing problems that need to be addressed [55,56]. Therefore, developing new therapeutic strategies and combination therapies is a major focus of current OSCC research. DDP is a widely used clinical anticancer drug with broad-spectrum antitumor activity. DDP works by binding to DNA bases, forming DNA adducts that inhibit DNA replication and transcription, ultimately leading to cancer cell death [57]. Although DDP is effective against various cancers, its use in OSCC treatment faces challenges. Firstly, DDP lacks targeting specificity, causing damage to normal cells and leading to severe side effects. Secondly, cancer cell resistance to DDP remains an issue that needs to be resolved [58]. Thus, improving the targeting specificity of DDP and reducing its side effects are key areas of research.

HA, an endogenous polysaccharide biomolecule present in human tissues, with excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability. HA has broad application potential in the field of nanomaterials, particularly in constructing nanodrug delivery systems [59–61]. By using HA as a carbon source to prepare HA-CDs, its excellent fluorescence properties and drug-loading capabilities can be utilized for targeted OSCC therapy. HA-CDs not only possess superior hydrophilicity but also can effectively bind to the anticancer drug DDP through surface functional groups like hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups, forming HA-CDs-DDP nanocomposites. These nanocomposites can target tumor sites in vivo, increasing drug concentration within tumor cells, thereby enhancing anticancer effects and reducing damage to normal tissues.

PDT employs the interaction between a light source and a PS to produce ROS or free radicals to kill cancer cells. During PDT, the PS transitions from the ground state to an excited state under irradiation with a specific wavelength of light, producing ROS or free radicals that damage the target tissue [62]. However, PDT faces challenges when treating solid tumors like OSCC. Firstly, the penetration depth of the light used in PDT is limited, affecting treatment efficacy. Secondly, the tumor microenvironment is often hypoxic, affecting ROS production and weakening PDT efficacy. Additionally, the high concentration of reduced glutathione (GSH) within tumor cells can deplete ROS generated by PDT, further reducing treatment effectiveness. Lastly, the limited targeting specificity, photostability, and ROS production capability of currently used PS molecules in clinical practice restrict PDT's application in solid tumor treatment [63,64]. To overcome these challenges, the development of nanotechnology in recent years has brought new opportunities for PDT. By combining HA-CDs with DDP to form HA-CDs-DDP nanocomposites, targeted therapy for OSCC can be achieved. These nanocomposites have the dual capability of accumulating at tumor sites via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect and producing significant amounts of ROS under laser irradiation, enhancing PDT efficacy.

In this study, we synthesized HA-CDs through a one-pot hydrothermal method and successfully loaded DDP onto HA-CDs using magnetic stirring, forming HA-CDs-DDP nanocomposites. Characterization tests showed that HA-CDs-DDP possessed functional moieties like CO, C-N, and C-O-C, with a drug loading rate of 61.05 % and a drug release rate of nearly 60 %, demonstrating HA-CDs' excellent capability as a DDP carrier. In laboratory experiments, it was observed that HA-CDs-DDP exhibited notable suppression of Cal-27 cell proliferation under laser irradiation, exhibiting good PDT effects. Furthermore, the in vivo study included the implantation of Cal-27 cells beneath the skin of nude mice, followed by the administration of HA-CDs, HA-CDs-DDP, and DDP via tail vein injection, combined with laser irradiation for treatment. The findings indicated that the treatment effect was most significant in the HA-CDs-DDP + laser group, where the tumor almost completely disappearing. The treatment effects in the HA-CDs + laser group and the DDP + laser group were slightly inferior to those in the HA-CDs-DDP + laser group, demonstrating that combining PDT with chemotherapy can more effectively treat OSCC.

Despite the significant potential of HA-CDs-DDP in OSCC treatment, many issues need to be addressed. Firstly, further optimization of HA-CDs synthesis methods and drug-loading techniques is necessary to improve drug loading rates and release efficiencies. Secondly, the metabolic pathways and biosafety of HA-CDs in vivo need to be thoroughly investigated to ensure their long-term safety. Additionally, further in vivo studies and clinical trials are imperative to validate their effectiveness and safety in humans. Subsequent research endeavors should also concentrate on amalgamating nanomaterials in PDT alongside other therapeutic modalities like immunotherapy and gene therapy, to form comprehensive treatment strategies that enhance OSCC treatment outcomes. Moreover, the application of nanomaterials in early diagnosis and real-time monitoring should be explored to achieve comprehensive management and precise treatment of OSCC. In conclusion, the HA-CDs-DDP system presents a fresh and efficacious approach for treating OSCC. With the continuous advancement of nanotechnology and biomedical research, this system is poised to assume a pivotal role in future cancer treatments, bringing more hope and benefits to patients.

In summary, the HA-CDs-DDP nanocomposite material we prepared exhibits excellent biocompatibility. Compared to previous work, our primary focus was on the ability of HA-CDs-DDP to selectively target cancer cells and induce apoptosis through PDT. In the current study, we combined HA-CDs with cisplatin to create a dual-functional nanocomposite that not only serves as a photosensitizer but also enhances chemotherapy efficacy by improving drug delivery and increasing ROS generation. We have now included molecular and animal-level experiments to further validate the feasibility of HA as a photosensitizer. In vitro and in vivo experiments suggest that the nanocomposite material not only specifically recognizes Cal-27 cells for precise delivery of the chemotherapeutic drug DDP but also has the effect of PDT, generating ROS under 660 nm laser irradiation. This process more effectively kills tumors and overcomes limitations of single treatments. This may provide new opportunities for optimizing combined therapeutic strategies based on chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy, leading to more promising clinical cancer treatment.Today, after decades of development, targeted chemotherapy has been successfully applied in clinical treatment, becoming an alternative to surgery in some cancer treatments. However, tumor cells are active cells, so they react to external treatments in their own way. This leads to the development of resistance to some of our targeted therapeutic drugs over time. Once resistance occurs, the effectiveness of this treatment is significantly compromised, necessitating a switch to another new targeted drug. In order to address these issues and improve efficacy, researchers are combining chemotherapy with PDT, demonstrating potential prospects in clinical settings. Although the combined treatment of targeted chemotherapy and PDT is less common in clinical applications, we are confident that the emergence of combination therapy will become a successful component of future cancer treatments.

ce:section.ce:para: The animal experiments adhered to the guidelines outlined in the "Regulations on the Management of Laboratory Animals" and the "Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals" from Shanxi Province. These experiments were undertaken with explicit approval of the Ethics Committee at Shanxi Medical University. Approval number is KQDW-2024–002.
